speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 13th, 1996. Survivor's name is Gary Matzdorff. Interviewer is Debbie Helman. The interview is being done in Ventura, California, United States. It will be conducted in English. My name is Debbie Helman. Today's date is August 13th, 1996. I am interviewing Gary Matzdorff. We are in the city of Ventura, California, United States of America. This interview will be conducted in English. Mr. Matzdorff, would you state your name and spell it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Gary Matzdorff. G-A-R-Y M-A-T-Z-D-O-R-F-F, like in Frank.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- my name at birth was Gunter Matzdorff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-U-umlaut-N-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born July 10th, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Herbert, and my mother's name, Edith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born in Breslau, Germany, Silesia. My mother was born in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never had any.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages were spoken in your home as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. Only German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only German? What type of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a fur expert, and importer, and a salesman in fur and fur, fur products.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: At times, later on during the Hitler period. She sewed lampshades. She learned how to sew, make lampshades, and to augment the family income.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your Jewish family life growing up in Berlin. In other words, what kind of things did your family do for Jewish holidays and your early memories of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we've always gone to the synagogue during the high holidays, occasionally, also for Passover and Hanukkah. But never failed to go to the synagogue on the high holidays. I was bar mitzvah at 13. I was active later on in the synagogue because of the school I went to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. What about Friday nights in your home? Does that have any memory for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We would-- occasionally, we would light candles and have a challah. But that was not the norm. Later on in life we did. In Shanghai, we did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the relationship between you and your non-Jewish friends in Berlin as you grew up in the late '20s and '30s.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know the difference. I had many friends and really didn't know whether they weren't Jewish or not. That was brought home later on, perhaps in my teens or early teens, when I became more aware that I was Jewish and some of my friends were not. But I had many non-Jewish friends, even until Hitler came to power and even shortly after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you experience any anti-Semitism as a child or a teenager?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- the first time I experienced it was in 1934 when a group of us, friends and cousins, were in a organization that I belonged to. It was like a-- Boy Scouts, similar to Boy Scouts but Jewish. And when we were attacked by Nazis during our camp out, camp-- camp out. And although my mother didn't want me to go to begin with, because she felt it was not the right time, it was dangerous. But you know how kids are. I went anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: What became the first indication of danger for you towards the end of '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, the-- the anxiety grew during the '30s. We knew that-- or I knew-- that the couldn't be any further existence in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: The discussion around the dinner table and in the clubs, different meetings, was always about immigration. Where to go, how to go. And heard my parents talk to their friends continuously about leaving Germany. But the question was, how and where to? How did your life begin to change once it was established how you would leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a gradual process.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the process, and how you were able to learn of the, the possibilities of going to Shanghai.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, the fear of not being able to leave and not knowing where to go until the discussion focused on Shanghai. And my parents tried to make arrangements to go to Shanghai. My father somehow heard about it, that we-- you don't need a visa to enter Shanghai. So the process started. The question was how to pay for it. And those are my memories. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. It's all right. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: The only question was-- well, my dad went to the Jewish organization. [CRYING] He had enough money to pay for two tickets, and he needed to have the money for two more for my grandmother. We were four. The-- there was a lot of anxiety because my grandmother didn't want to be a burden. She didn't want to go. And my mother said she's not going to leave without her. But eventually, my dad did receive the money from the-- his fine. And got the tickets to leave in October of 1938. But at that time, he did not get his exit visa on time for some reason. And we finally got another booking in April of '39. So consequently, I went through the Crystal Night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was an apprentice. I started my apprenticeship when I was 14 and a half, in 1936. And that day--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what field was your apprenticeship?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned the leather trade as a craftsman. And I-- that day was my day for a trade school. Once a week, every apprentice had to go to a trade school. And I-- that was, that was my day. And I believe it was on a Thursday. And in the morning on the bus, I already noticed roving bands smashing store windows, some others painting the word jude on the window, and then the others would smash the windows. And so I sort of crawled in the back of the bus. And I had to change over to a street car to get to, to the trade school. And there was a-- I-- there was a fire in a backyard of a-- in a courtyard of a, of a building.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stopped briefly and asked some people what's going on. There was no fire department. Oh, somebody said, ah, some Jew house is burning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And apparently, it was a small synagogue, orthodox synagogue, in the, in the courtyard. By watching that for a moment, I, I was tardy in school. Opened the classroom, I was the only Jewish student in school. And books started flying towards me as I opened the door. So I closed the door again, and just walked a corridor for a minute, or stood there stunned. When the door opened, the teacher came out and handed me the box. And he said, I had to do this for the class. I was the only student, I believe I mentioned that. The only Jewish student in class. And he said, get a hold of your father real quick and try to get out of Germany as quickly as possible. Terrible things are going to happen. Good luck. And that was it. I went downstairs to a phone booth, called home. But my father was already on his way to his office and mother didn't know anything about it. So I waited another half hour, 45 minutes, and called my dad in his office. And he saw the same thing. He says, well, call me back in about an hour, see, see if I can make some arrangements, which I did. In the meantime, my father got a hold of a friend of his who is not Jewish, and he was a customer as well. And made some arrangements. He said, meet me at a certain subway station, and then we'll discuss where we're going to go. He said he doesn't want to mention anything over the phone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we met and we rode the subway all day long. When we saw somebody that looked like gestapo, the typical short, cropped hair cut look, we got off the train, walked over to the other side, and went the other direction.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fortunately, we were never caught during that day. And since it was November, it got dark early. Then we finally slipped into his friend's apartment. And-- which was right in the center of the city, as a matter of fact. And we stayed there for two weeks, my dad and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that family?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Mr. and Mrs. Ziska. Z-I-S-K-A. And we never went down, downstairs or anything. We watched some of the parades even from their window, because they were right in the center of town. Fortunately, they never came to our apartment because they were rounding up Jews wherever they found them, in the streets. Anybody who looked Jewish, we all needed to have identification cards.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was very easy once we showed the card that we were Jews, and then were hauled away, at that time, to the police station, and then jails.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no concentration camp. There was no-- except that we knew of concentration camps, but they were not yet extermination camps. There were concentration camps where people who had acted against the laws of the Third Reich, which was trumped up charges anyway, most of the time. But they will hauled, at that time, to the police station, in order to-- and that is strictly my observation. At that time, the Germans did not want the bodies. They just wanted the people to leave and leave their belongings. Their apartments, their business, their cars, their, their furniture, whatever they owned. And that was a way of forcing the wives to make all the arrangements. And once they showed that they have exit visas and they had the passport for those who were in jail, that they could-- they were then let out to leave. Some of them I saw him on board ship. They still came with shaven hair, right straight from the, from jail. But fortunately, like I said, we were never caught. Two weeks later, everything simmered down and we finally went back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you able to stay in your home until the final plans to exit were made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we left in April of '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so it was several months, and I finished my, my-- actually, I went back to work and finished my apprenticeship on January 6, 1939. And-- but as a Jew, I was not allowed to make my exam. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was not permitted, to make my journeyman exam.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We'll start once again. We've eliminated a sound. Tell me more about your plans to exit Germany, how the arrangements were made, and how they were made, with the help of--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. To the best of my recollection, my dad tried to obtain the, the necessary financing for the additional two tickets, and-- because we needed four and he had enough money for two.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went up to the [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish organization or HIAS, as we know it now. And he was asked-- actually, at that time, this was about July, August of 1938-- what are you going to do in Shanghai? You can't make a living there. There's nobody there. So he said, well, look. I play the piano as a hobby. If worse comes to worse, I can go to a-- it's a honky-tonk town. Probably find work in a bar to play the piano. So they still wouldn't give it to him. He said, show us that you know how to play the piano. And I remember my dad had to go down to some place where somebody listened to him that he did know how to play the piano.

speaker: interviewee
text: Based on that, he was able to get the money. And well, the rest is history. We left in April on a, a trip to freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was left behind of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents on my father's side, both Otto and Sarah in Breslau. They were old people. They were up in age, had no intentions of leaving, too afraid to, to, to leave their homes. So they were the ones-- that was the immediate family-- that we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any brothers and sisters of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents never had any brothers or sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the date you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mid April. The exact date I don't remember. I think it was around the 20th of April, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What method of transportation took you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took the train from Berlin to Italy, Genoa, boarded the ship, the SS Victoria. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And got into Shanghai, May of 1939. So it took about one month.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, just about.

speaker: interviewer
text: To do the journey. How were you received when you arrived in Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On board ship, life was still very nice, although we traveled economic class. It was quite a shock. We were assigned to-- on board ship, we were assigned to a camp. In our case, it was called Kinchow Road Camp, which was a school that was converted into a camp. We were loaded on trucks and transported to the schools. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Under whose supervision was this done?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was done through the Jewish organizations who waited for us in Shanghai. So there was more help than, that we expected to be there. Some of the Russian Jews apparently organized. There was-- I remember also a Hungarian Jew by the name of [? Como, ?] I believe his name was, who had a committee that helped refugees to come in. Because the ships unloaded almost every day from, from Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live once you arrived in Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in the Kinchow Road Camp for about a month. And then my dad received $200 through HIAS. And with that money, he rented an apartment, an apartment. One room, and we all lived in this one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was with you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother, my mother, Dad, and I. That was my mother's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your treatment by the authorities, and who were the authorities there over you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were the Chinese, of course. Shanghai was an international settlement. And because of that status, there were no visa requirements. The international settlement where we landed, there was a French town, or French concession, with French police, with French courts, even French street names. And then there was Hongkew. Hongkew was already occupied by the Japanese since 1937, during the Sino-Japanese war. Which I understand, that sector was once an American concession or sector. And so we were brought into Hongkew, where this Kinchow Road Camp was and other camps were. And it was the cheapest neighborhood in the city to, to settle down and find a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were able to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was 18. And my first job was with a-- as a secretary, private secretary, to a Eurasian man. And his name was T.R. Crank. I worked for him for six months. But once he found out that I learned to speak Chinese from one of the employees, he fired me. He didn't like that I learned the language so I wouldn't find out what his business was. And after that, I got a job as a broker for several firms. And the last one, before the war broke out, was with a Dutch firm called Sino-Java Import and Export Firm. And I was hired on a-- first, on a trial basis. But even that never finished because November 10th, as it was for us, the-- December, December 8th-- I'm sorry. December 8th, Pearl Harbor broke out, and that was the end of that. And then they, they let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as you worked, your-- you made money and you were able to buy food and things your family needed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within the ghetto itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that area. At that time, it was still not considered a ghetto. It was just a, a poor district of Shanghai. Yes. We always-- I've always worked in different capacities. I-- my father also had-- started an import business in fur, which could have been very successful if it wasn't for Pearl Harbor. And that shut everything off. And the-- in, I believe, in mid '43, the Japanese had a proclamation-- actually, it came out of '42-- that by a certain date, all status refugees had to move into the district, or ghetto, as we now call it. And that was Hongkew. And then they put barbed wire around it. And people who lived outside, prior to that, who had businesses, had to either close them and move into the, into the ghetto area. And there we stayed until the end of the war, until 1945. That's when life was difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: During that period, during the occupation of the Japanese. Because the Japanese immediately occupied the whole city, walked across Garden Bridge and occupied Frenchtown and total city. And they were, they were in charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how that began, the Japanese occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- there were American gunboats in the harbor. And I believe, I believe the name of one was the Wakefield. I'm not sure. I think they sank themselves. One ship, I think, escaped, a British ship, I believe. But it was very swift. The Japanese moved right in the next morning, it was all apparently pre-planned. The-- all the British, American, and the ABC, all the Dutch and the French, and all the aliens were eventually rounded up. They had to wear armbands. And we could still-- actually, in '41, we was-- '42, we're still able to go into the city. But then eventually, when barbed wire was put around the district, unless you had a permit and you had to go to a police station. The man in charge of that police station was-- his name was Ghoya. Very much feared by the immigrants. He called himself the King of the Jews. And he was a little man. And if he didn't like your face, he wouldn't give you a permit to go into the city, because some people had jobs outside of the district and needed to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or if you were a particularly tall person, he would jump up on a chair and slap you. He had a inferiority complex, the man. Later on, he claimed that he protected the Jews from extermination by acting the way he did. But who knows? So were you restricted within the ghetto at that point? And what other people were in the ghetto of Hongkew with you? Were there only Jewish immigrants?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We lived in a Chinese neighborhood, occupied by Chinese. But eventually, all 20,000 status refugees were living in the ghetto, in the district. The majority had lived there, even before the war, and the rest filled up. We also had a very strong Jewish life because members of a [NON-ENGLISH] from Poland came to Shanghai via Japan, [INAUDIBLE]. They were on the way to the United States, but was stopped there because of the war. And then they were transported to Shanghai, to Hongkew. And they give a tremendous sense of Jewish life and Judaism to the district, to the Jews who lived there. We were not in danger, in a sense, unless there were infractions. One example, for instance, I remember was we were standing on a street corner and just talking. Friend of mine had a bicycle. And the rear end of his bicycle was partially in the, in the roadway. A Japanese lorry or a Japanese truck came by, hit his bicycle. And he made a big fuss. My friend made a big fuss. The truck stopped. The driver and his companion came out in uniform. And of course, not a word of English. And he said he wants it repaired. He said, OK. Put your bike on the back of the truck, and come with us. We've never heard, seen him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those kind of things happened. Immigrants were thrown into jail for minor infractions, I believe. And so if you were-- if you paid attention and, and stayed clean, you weren't bothered by them. OK. We're going to stop now to change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
